issues.title: `KeyEventResult.skipRemainingHandlers` works differently on Desktop and Web.
issues.body:
<!-- Thank you for using Flutter!

     If you are looking for support, please check out our documentation
     or consider asking a question on Stack Overflow:
      * https://flutter.dev/
      * https://api.flutter.dev/
      * https://stackoverflow.com/questions/tagged/flutter?sort=frequent

     If you have found a bug or if our documentation doesn't have an answer
     to what you're looking for, then fill out the template below. Please read
     our guide to filing a bug first: https://flutter.dev/docs/resources/bug-reports
-->

## Steps to Reproduce

<!-- Please include full steps to reproduce so that we can reproduce the problem. -->

Give focus to the TextField and input text, arrow keys, backspace key, etc. with the keyboard.

**Expected results:** <!-- what did you want to see? -->

Text input, direction keys, and backspace key should work.

**Actual results:** <!-- what did you see? -->

web : Text input is also possible, and the arrow keys and backspace work well.
Windows: Text can be entered, but the arrow keys and backspace do not work.

<details>
<summary>Code sample</summary>

<!--
      Please create a minimal reproducible sample that shows the problem
      and attach it below between the lines with the backticks.

      To create it you can use `flutter create bug` command and update the `main.dart` file.

      Alternatively, you can use https://dartpad.dev/
      which is capable of creating and running small Flutter apps.

      Without this we will unlikely be able to progress on the issue, and because of that
      we regretfully will have to close it.
-->

```dart
class _DevelopmentScreenState extends State<DevelopmentScreen> {
  FocusNode focus = FocusNode(onKeyEvent: (FocusNode node, KeyEvent event) {
    return KeyEventResult.skipRemainingHandlers;
  });

  @override
  Widget build(BuildContext context) {
    return Scaffold(
      body: FocusScope(
        onKey: (_, __) {
          return KeyEventResult.handled;
        },
        child: Container(
          padding: const EdgeInsets.all(15),
          child: TextField(
            focusNode: focus,
          ),
        ),
      ),
    );
  }
}
```

</details>

<details>

Hi @bosskmk, Thanks for filing the issue, I can replicate the issue, running the below code sample arrow keys and backspace keypress are not responding when running on Desktop (verified on Mac/Windows) but works on Web.


<details>
<summary>complete code sample</summary>

```dart
import 'package:flutter/material.dart';

void main() => runApp(MyApp());

class MyApp extends StatelessWidget {
  @override
  Widget build(BuildContext context) {
    return MaterialApp(
```

cc @gspencergoog @LongCatIsLooong 
I think this is the consequence of handling text actions at the outermost of the app. The events are consumed by the Focus and skipped. The behavior is intended but confusing.

This is because the focus node on the text field is telling the focus system "stop propagating key events and send them back to the engine unhandled" before the key editing shortcuts (defined in the top level `WidgetsApp` `Shortcuts` widget) can be seen by the top level definition to be handled.

With that in place, the `FocusScope` part of the sample is irrelevant: no events ever get to it.  If the text field didn't have a focus node that was intercepting all the events, then the `FocusScope` as defined would stop all event propagation, and there wouldn't even be working text input. The only reason there is any text input is because when the engine receives an unhandled event back, it still passes it to the text input handlers there, but they don't handle the editing commands because they need to be overridable by Dart code. On the web, if I remember correctly, we use a hidden native HTML text field so that browser extensions, etc., will work properly, so we can't intercept the editing keys on text fields quite as early there.

I'm not sure what to do about this issue: we do want to define the text editing commands at the top level so that they can be overridden globally, but it does mean that if something stops propagation of text editing commands in the middle, they won't execute (which is expected). I'm inclined to just close this as "as designed", but I'd like to know more about what @bosskmk was trying to achieve here first.

@gspencergoog 
The behavior of KeyEventResult.skipRemainingHandlers works well in Web.

But KeyEventResult.skipRemainingHandlers does not work on desktop.

If KeyEventResult.skipRemainingHandlers is returned as the onKey return value from TextField, the onKey callback of the parent FocusNode is not called, but a key input must be made in the TextField.

Currently, input of characters in the above sample code works as expected, but only the arrow keys and backspace keys work differently.

The behavior in the current web version is
If KeyEventResult.skipRemainingHandlers is returned as the return value of the onKey callback of TextField, the onKey callback of the parent FocusNode is not called, and key actions such as text, arrow keys, and backspace are transmitted.

This is the expected value when returning KeyEventResult.skipRemainingHandlers , and we want it to behave that way on Desktop as well.
I've fixed this on my own in a sloppy way for now, but hopefully it will be officially updated.

In our current design, the web is actually the one that is incorrect, and the desktop platforms are operating correctly. If we could stop the web text field from handling actions in that case and override them, we would, because it would allow more complete overriding capabilities for web.

I am curious, though, what is your use case? Why do you want to have this configuration where key events are blocked in this way? It might help inform future design decisions if we know what you're trying to achieve, and there also might be a better solution we could suggest.

My package is hierarchically detecting keyboard events.

It has a TextField widget and keyboard events that should only be detected in this part.

And there are cases where it needs to detect keyboard events in the parent's hierarchy.

In this hierarchical structure, sometimes my widget needs to intercept keyboard events on keystrokes in the TextField to trigger them, and sometimes I just need to pass the keystrokes to the TextField.

If the behavior of KeyEventResult.skipRemainingHandlers is normal in Desktop, what is the difference between the behavior of KeyEventResult.skipRemainingHandlers and KeyEventResult.handled in the Desktop version?

In the web version, the two operation methods are different.
KeyEventResult.handled will stop all keystroke events and KeyEventResult.skipRemainingHandlers will stop the execution of handlers but pass events to platform . I think this is the correct behavior.

The translator is great, but I don't know if my writing will be delivered well.

My pakage. https://pub.dev/packages/pluto_grid

OK, I think I see. The translation seems to be good. I hope it works well in both directions. :-)

> If the behavior of KeyEventResult.skipRemainingHandlers is normal in Desktop, what is the difference between the behavior of KeyEventResult.skipRemainingHandlers and KeyEventResult.handled in the Desktop version?

The difference between these flags is supposed to be that if the Flutter view is embedded as part of a larger application which also includes native elements, those native elements will get a chance to handle the key events if `skipRemainingHandlers` is used, and will not see them if `handled` is used. The `skipRemainingHandlers` value is used to implement the [`modal` ](https://api.flutter.dev/flutter/widgets/ShortcutManager/modal.html) flag for  the `ShortcutManager`, allowing it to be used to block or replace inherited shortcuts for its descendants.

> In this hierarchical structure, sometimes my widget needs to intercept keyboard events on keystrokes in the TextField to trigger them, and sometimes I just need to pass the keystrokes to the TextField.

So why isn't it sufficient to return `handled` from `onKey` when you need to intercept them, and `ignored` when you don't? If you don't handle them, they will be passed to the text field. I think maybe I'm missing something here.

If handled as a response value is returned, the key cannot be passed to the TextField.

Therefore, the parent's callback is not called by responding to skipRemainingHandlers, but it is passed to the TextField.

I'm not sure if this is the correct behavior.

However, since I first developed on the web, I expected the same behavior, and it was not, so I left an issue.

I would like Desktop to work in the same way as Web works, but I think it is unavoidable if it is not.

I'll check the use of ShortcutManager .

thank you.
It made sense to open an issue on this, thanks.  I'm not sure what we can do about it, but there is a difference in the handling of the result.

I would like to ask if there has been any progress?

> I've fixed this on my own in a sloppy way for now, but hopefully it will be officially updated.
Would you please share your workaround?
The `triaged-desktop` label is irrelevant if there is no `team-desktop` label or `fyi-desktop` label.

SOLUTION that worked for me. In my case, I have changed amountTextController.text value in OnChanged() function. This created issue of text not showing in textfield after backspace as it continuously changed the value of text controller in recursive. To avoid this issue, give proper condition statements to break the recursive. 
Hope It will work for others.

If you are using Frozen keyboard and facing this issue, this issue can be solved by doing the following things:
1) Go to frozen keyboard app -> Language -> turn off Unicode switch

* If you are using Unicode font, you have to turn off Auto detect keyboard switch too because auto detect keyboard setting automatically switch on Unicode if you are using Unicode.

A few notes from my initial investigation. `beginBatchEdit/endBatchEdit` are methods contained in the Android embedder `InputConnectionAdaptor` implementation that are used to accumulate a series of changes in a text input component so they can be processed as a single batch.

On a Flutter App:

When pressing the backspace on the frozen keyboard, a batch edit is started and a keydown event is dispatched. The batch edit is not ended by a call to `endBatchEdit()`.
When pressing the backspace on a hardware keyboard, a keydown/keyup event is dispatched. No batch edit started.
When pressing backspace on Gboard in an empty field, a keydown/keyup event is dispatched. No batch edit started.

On native Android App:

When pressing the backspace on the frozen keyboard, a batch edit is started and a keydown event is dispatched. The batch edit is not ended by a call to `endBatchEdit()`.
When pressing backspace on hardware keyboard, a keydown/keyup event is dispatched. A batch edit is started/ended before the key up event is dispatched.
On Gboard when pressing a backspace on an empty field, a keydown/keyup event is dispatched. A batch edit is started/ended before the key up event is dispatched.

The reason for input not working after a backspace on the frozen keyboard is because the call to `beginBatchEdit` is not followed by a matching call to `endBatchEdit`, so the `ListenableEditableState` does not notify its listeners of any changes.

Seems partially related to https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/122441#issuecomment-1532983639 . It looks like some keyboard implementations might not dispatch a keyup event for a previous keydown event, in this case for backspace, and  some implementations may trigger a `beginBatchEdit` without a matching `endBatchEdit`. Though even if this is the case native Android seems to handle this gracefully and still allow for input.

---
**Reproduce screen recording**

https://github.com/flutter/flutter/assets/39775137/25c8b3d0-4552-4d97-9d79-82f8a44e4f8c
---
**Edit solution**
As my opinion, solve by listening keypress detection and  request focus node for now. But I don't know what can cause as consequences. If think can cause error or reduce quality performance please show me.

```dart
class _KeyboardFixPageState extends State<KeyboardFixPage> {
  
  final TextEditingController _cacheUserIdCtrl = TextEditingController();

  final TextEditingController _secondCtrl = TextEditingController();

  final fc = FocusNode();
  final inputFieldFc = FocusNode();

  @override
  Widget build(BuildContext context) {
    return Scaffold(
      appBar: AppBar(
        title: const Text('Keyboard bug'),
      ),
      body: RawKeyboardListener(
        focusNode: fc,
        autofocus: true,
        onKey: (event){
          if(event.isKeyPressed(LogicalKeyboardKey.backspace)){
            if(event is RawKeyDownEvent){
               inputFieldFc.unfocus();
                Future.delayed( Duration(milliseconds: 1), () {
                  inputFieldFc.requestFocus();
                });
            }
          }
          else{
            print('No!');
          }
        },
        child: Center(
          child: Column(
            children: [
              TextFormField(
                keyboardType: TextInputType.number,
                controller: _cacheUserIdCtrl,
                maxLength: 8,
                textInputAction: TextInputAction.next,
                textAlignVertical: TextAlignVertical.center,
                focusNode: inputFieldFc,
                style: const TextStyle(
                  color: Color(0xff474747),
                  fontSize: 15.6,
                  fontWeight: FontWeight.w400,
                  letterSpacing: 0.6,
                ),
              ),
              TextFormField(
                keyboardType: TextInputType.number,
                controller: _secondCtrl,
                maxLength: 8,
                textInputAction: TextInputAction.next,
                textAlignVertical: TextAlignVertical.center,
                style: const TextStyle(
                  color: Color(0xff474747),
                  fontSize: 15.6,
                  fontWeight: FontWeight.w400,
                  letterSpacing: 0.6,
                ),
              ),
            ],
          ),
        ),
      ),
    );
  }
}
```

I think the bugs is because of **Frozen** Keyboard. Bugs should fix by frozen keyboard developer. This issue occurred when the frozen keyboard refocus itself after delete key pressed. It's working fine in android native EditText. So I create EditText in android native and invoked it. That is working fine smoothly. Later I found the library for the use of native text input. 

**_[https://pub.dev/packages/flutter_native_text_input](https://pub.dev/packages/flutter_native_text_input)_**

Just use that library to save time. 🍻
@SanKoKo Work smoothly but not contain fully function like decoration for focus text field and others.

issues.title: [Web] Select word for suggestions box using "Tab" key but changes focus
issues.body:
Trying to select a word that is suggestion with the tab key, it behaves as if it focuses on another text field.
This is video I captured


https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/7422251/113574888-f9ab3400-9657-11eb-9fd8-a24bc3ddff78.mp4



## Steps to Reproduce

This is minimum code

<details>
<summary>code sample</summary>

```dart
import 'package:flutter/material.dart';
import 'package:flutter/scheduler.dart';

class SamplePage extends StatefulWidget {

  SamplePage(): super();

  @override
  SamplePageState createState() => SamplePageState();
}

class SamplePageState extends State<SamplePage> with WidgetsBindingObserver {
  ScrollController _scrollController = ScrollController();

  @override
  void initState(){
    super.initState();
    SchedulerBinding.instance?.addPostFrameCallback((_){
      setState(() { });
    });    
  }    

  Widget build(BuildContext context){
    List<Widget> widgetList = [
      Center(child: TextField(maxLines: null, ),),
      Divider(),
      Center(child: TextField(maxLines: null, ),),
      Divider(),
    ];

    Widget bodyScaffold = Scaffold(
      body: Scrollbar(
        controller : _scrollController, 
        child      : SingleChildScrollView(
          controller  : _scrollController,
          child       : Column(
            children : widgetList,
          )
        )
      )
    );

    return bodyScaffold;
  }
}
```
</details>




1. Run `flutter run -d chrome `.
2. Input text in upper TextField. ( I used japanese )
3. Use tab key to select word from suggestion box.

**Expected results:**
Not change focus to other text field and select word from suggestion box

**Actual results:**
Focus changes to other text field and the first suggestion is selected

  <summary>Logs</summary>

Hi @hatano0x06 
Your code does not give any suggestion while typing in the text fields!

I can reproduce this with Japanese keyboard. Tested on latest stable and master channel. 

### Flutter behavior

issues.title: Hero animation on text field dismiss keyboard
issues.body:
<!-- Thank you for using Flutter!

     If you are looking for support, please check out our documentation
     or consider asking a question on Stack Overflow:
      * https://flutter.dev/
      * https://api.flutter.dev/
      * https://stackoverflow.com/questions/tagged/flutter?sort=frequent

     If you have found a bug or if our documentation doesn't have an answer
     to what you're looking for, then fill out the template below. Please read
     our guide to filing a bug first: https://flutter.dev/docs/resources/bug-reports
-->

## Steps to Reproduce

<!-- Please include full steps to reproduce so that we can reproduce the problem. -->

1. Execute `flutter run` on the code sample <!-- (see "Code sample" section below) -->
2. Click on the text field on the home screen. <!-- describe steps to demonstrate bug -->
3. See keyboard up and down and up again on the second screen<!-- for example "Tap on X and see a crash" -->

**Expected results:** <!-- what did you want to see? -->
The keyboard persists after hero animation is finished without jumping up and down. 

**Actual results:** <!-- what did you see? -->
The keyboard dismissed once but because of autofocus its up again. Some times the keyboard was dismissed twice 

<details>
<summary>Code Sample</summary>

<!--
      Please create a minimal reproducible sample that shows the problem
      and attach it below between the lines with the backticks.

      To create it you can use `flutter create bug` command and update the `main.dart` file.

      Alternatively, you can use https://dartpad.dev/
      which is capable of creating and running small Flutter apps.

      Without this we will unlikely be able to progress on the issue, and because of that
      we regretfully will have to close it.
-->
```import 'package:flutter/material.dart';

void main() {
  runApp(const MyApp());
}

class MyApp extends StatelessWidget {
  const MyApp({Key? key}) : super(key: key);

  // This widget is the root of your application.
  @override
  Widget build(BuildContext context) {
    return MaterialApp(
      title: 'Flutter Demo',
      theme: ThemeData(
        primarySwatch: Colors.blue,
      ),
      routes: {
        // When navigating to the "/" route, build the FirstScreen widget.
        'home': (context) => const MyHomePage(),
        'search': (context) => const Search(),
      },
      home:  const MyHomePage(),
    );
  }
}

class MyHomePage extends StatefulWidget {
  
  const MyHomePage({Key? key}) : super(key: key);
  
  @override
  State<MyHomePage> createState() => _MyHomePageState();
}

class _MyHomePageState extends State<MyHomePage> {

  @override
  Widget build(BuildContext context) {

    Size size = MediaQuery.of(context).size;

    return Scaffold(
        backgroundColor: Colors.white,
        appBar: AppBar(
          title: const Text('Home',style: TextStyle(color: Colors.white),),
          flexibleSpace: Container(),
          actions: [IconButton(icon: const Icon(Icons.favorite_border,),onPressed: (){})],
          bottom: PreferredSize(
            preferredSize: Size.fromHeight(size.height * 0.07),
            child:  Padding(
              padding: const EdgeInsets.only(left:10.0,right: 10.0, bottom:10.0),
              child: SizedBox(
                height:size.height * 0.06,
                child: Hero(
                  tag:'searchBar',
                  child: Material(
                    type: MaterialType.transparency,
                    child: SizedBox(
                      child: TextField(
                        onTap: ()=>Navigator.pushNamed(context,'search'),
                        readOnly: true,
                        autofocus:false,
                        decoration: InputDecoration(
                          prefixIcon: const Icon(Icons.search, size: 25.0,),
                          border: OutlineInputBorder(
                            borderRadius: BorderRadius.circular(50.0),
                            borderSide: const BorderSide(
                              width: 0,
                              style: BorderStyle.none,
                            ),
                          ),
                          filled: true,
                          hintStyle: TextStyle(color: Colors.grey[800]),
                          hintText: 'Search a word',
                          fillColor: Colors.white,
                          isDense: true,
                          contentPadding: const EdgeInsets.all(0.0),
                        ),
                      ),
                    ),
                  ),
                ),
              ),
            ),
          ),
        ),
        body: Container()
    );
  }
}

class Search extends StatefulWidget {
  const Search({Key? key}) : super(key: key);

  @override
  State<Search> createState() => _SearchState();
}

class _SearchState extends State<Search> {
  @override
  Widget build(BuildContext context) {

    Size size = MediaQuery.of(context).size;

    return Scaffold(
      appBar: AppBar(
        title: Hero(
          tag:'searchBar',
          child: Material(
            type: MaterialType.transparency,
            child: SizedBox(
              height:size.height * 0.05,
              child: TextField(
                // focusNode: focusNode,
                autofocus: true,
                decoration: InputDecoration(
                  prefixIcon: const Icon(Icons.search, size: 25.0,),
                  border: OutlineInputBorder(
                    borderRadius: BorderRadius.circular(50.0),
                    borderSide: const BorderSide(
                      width: 0,
                      style: BorderStyle.none,
                    ),
                  ),
                  filled: true,
                  hintStyle: TextStyle(color: Colors.grey[800]),
                  hintText: 'Search a word',
                  fillColor: Colors.white,
                  // isDense: true,
                  contentPadding: const EdgeInsets.all(0.0),
                ),
              ),
            ),
          ),
        ),
      ),
      body: Container(),
    );
  }
}

```

Yep, that seems like a problem.  I'm curious why the focus ever leaves the field in the hero, it seems to me that if a hero is focused, it should just stay focused for the entire transition.
any status how to resolve this?

Hero + autofocus = 💔

issues.title: TextField doesn't appear within a direction:Axis.vertical Wrap
comments:
Issue still exist when using `direction: Axis.vertical` but no issue when using `direction: Axis.horizontal`

### Code Sample
```dart
import 'package:flutter/material.dart';

void main() => runApp(MyApp());

class MyApp extends StatelessWidget {
  @override
  Widget build(BuildContext context) {
    return MaterialApp(
      title: 'Material App',
      home: Scaffold(
        appBar: AppBar(
          title: Text('Material App Bar'),
        ),
        body: Center(
          child: Wrap(
            direction: Axis.vertical,
            children: <Widget>[
              TextField(controller: TextEditingController(text: "123"))
            ],
          ),
        ),
      ),
    );
  }
}
```

issues.title: Text fields constrained to small sizes confuse the focus traversal algorithm
issues.body:
## Steps to Reproduce


**Code:**

```
import 'package:flutter/material.dart';

void main() {
  runApp(MyApp());
}

class MyApp extends StatelessWidget {
  @override
  Widget build(BuildContext context) {
    return MaterialApp(

      home: MyHomePage(),
    );
  }
}

class MyHomePage extends StatefulWidget {

  @override
  State<MyHomePage> createState() => _MyHomePageState();
}

class _MyHomePageState extends State<MyHomePage> {

  @override
  Widget build(BuildContext context) {

    return Scaffold(
        appBar: AppBar(
          // Here we take the value from the MyHomePage object that was created by
          // the App.build method, and use it to set our appbar title.
          title: Text("Test App"),
        ),
        body: Center(
          // Center is a layout widget. It takes a single child and positions it
          // in the middle of the parent.
            child:Container(
                height: 180,
                // width: MediaQuery.of(context).size.width * 0.9,
                child: GridView.count(
                    scrollDirection: Axis.vertical,
                    crossAxisCount: 4,
                    childAspectRatio: 5,
                    children:  [
                      buildColumn(context,txt: "A"),
                      buildColumn(context,txt: "B"),
                      buildColumn(context,txt: "C"),
                      buildColumn(context,txt: "D"),
                      buildColumn(context,txt: "E"),
                      buildColumn(context,txt: "F"),
                      buildColumn(context,txt: "G"),
                      buildColumn(context,txt: "H"),
                    ])
            )
        )
    );
  }

  Column buildColumn(BuildContext context,{txt}) {
    return Column(children: [
      Text(txt),
      Container(
          height: MediaQuery.of(context).size.height * 0.04,
          width: 300,
          child:TextFormField(decoration: InputDecoration(
            enabledBorder: OutlineInputBorder(
              borderSide: BorderSide(
                  color: Colors.black12, width: 1.0),
            ),),)
      ),
    ]
    );
  }
}
```


**Expected results:** 
Pressing the tab key should cycle through the text fields, left to right then down

**Actual results:** 
If the app screen is not maximised the tab key only cycles 2 fields in a continuous loop

Verified this on latest stable and seems to be occuring on desktop, as, the tab key keeps shifts focus between 2 textfields, while in maximized state, it traverses properly from all textfields.


https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/67046386/124481111-91d6c780-ddc5-11eb-856b-53df2927413b.mov

Although on web, the behavior is same while in maximized or not, ie, the tab key keeps shifting between 2 textfields in both states.

<details>
<summary> flutter doctor -v </summary>

```
[✓] Flutter (Channel stable, 2.2.3, on Mac OS X 10.15.4 19E2269 darwin-x64,
```

CC @gspencergoog

This probably has to do with the fields touching (or maybe overlapping?), and some place in the traversal algorithm that doesn't deal well with that.  I doubt it has anything to do with full screen mode. Adding a 1px padding to the fields would probably be a workaround for this.

I've tried adding the padding and still the same issue. The fields are not overlapping or touching as seen in pic. Even a few pixels less seems to makes a difference, my screenshot was only about 2 pixels less from full screen. Also,  it seems to be the same issue when I test the release on a screen smaller than the one I used when developing, even if I make it maximised
 
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/46990395/125024754-fc348400-e0b3-11eb-9d97-5d5caf3a22a0.png)
Great troubleshooting, thanks everyone. Understood, will use a fixed big enough height in the meantime.

This appears to be because the bounding rectangle for the widget with the focus node attached goes to zero height.  Not yet sure what to do about that, since clearly the text field itself isn't zero height.

This issue is assigned but has had no recent status updates. Please consider unassigning this issue if it is not going to be addressed in the near future. This allows people to have a clearer picture of what work is actually planned. Thanks!
Hi any updates on this? Having all sorts of overflows on small screen size due to having to fix the height
The `triaged-desktop` label is irrelevant if there is no `team-desktop` label or `fyi-desktop` label.

As Adam Savage would say: "Well, there's your problem!"

Although it's not your problem, it's Flutter's: we're translating the physical scan code for the remote as if it were a keyboard, and it's not, so the scan code is mapping to a "Control Right" even though it's not a control key. And the reason the assertion is firing is because although the physical key purports to be a control key, the corresponding metaState bit is not set.

And I'm not quite sure what to do here: the physical key mapping only makes sense when the input is a keyboard, but we use the physical key scancode to make sure that we match the appropriate key down with the right key up.  Perhaps we need to just not synchronize the modifiers if the source isn't a keyboard.
Do we need a separate set of mappings for IR remotes maybe? Or in general for lots of classes of devices? How does Android know what key this "really" is?

Android looks at the `.kl` keyboard layout files installed on the device for the peripheral, and they're different for different peripherals. These are not accessible to the app on a non-rooted device, as far as I know.

I think the right fix here is to have a way to tell if something is not a keyboard (which we should be able to do with platform APIs), and just skip the modifier key synchronization if it isn't a keyboard. Other devices don't use modifier key masks anyhow, so synchronization doesn't make sense.

I was able to make it work using @clarkap's solution, with the following changes :

- Removed the `event.deviceId != 3` condition, not sure what its purpose was but the doc for `deviceId` says :

> An id of zero indicates that the event didn't come from a physical device and maps to the default keymap. The other numbers are arbitrary and you shouldn't depend on the values.

- Replaced the `KeyEvent.KEYCODE_DPAD_CENTER` with `KeyEvent.KEYCODE_ENTER` in both `sendKeyEvent()`, because it doesn't correspond to any `LogicalKeyboardKey` and `HardwareKeyboard.instance.addHandler` didn't produce any event for it.
cc: @mariamhas
